scoreMatrix.desc: Tablets are one of the most common forms of HRT. You usually take them once a day. The fall in the amount of oestrogen your body produces can cause symptoms including hot flushes, low mood and vaginal dryness or irritation. Taking oestrogen as a form of HRT restores your levels of oestrogen and helps to relieve these symptoms. Taking tablets once a day may be the easiest way of having treatment. Some of the risks of HRT, such as blood clots, are higher with tablets than with patches, gel or spray (although the overall risk is still small).

Skin patches are a common way of taking HRT. They work by sticking onto your skin on the lower part of your body and they gradually release small amounts of hormones into your body. You'll usually change your patch every few days, but each brand is different. Patches may be a better option than tablets if you have difficulty swallowing tablets, or are likely to forget to take it. Using patches can also help to avoid some side effects of HRT, such as indigestion, and unlike tablets, they do not increase your risk of blood clots. You might find that skin patches do not always stick well, especially if you moisturise your skin. Patches can also cause redness or irritation, or leave a mark on the skin.

Low dose oestrogen is also available as a cream, gel, vaginal tablet, pessary or ring that you put inside your vagina. This can help with menopausal symptoms such as vaginal dryness, a burning sensation, or pain during sex. Vaginal oestrogen does not carry the usual risks of HRT and does not increase your risk of breast cancer. You can use it without taking progestogen, even if you still have a womb. This form of HRT will not help with other menopausal symptoms such as hot flushes, mood swings or sleeping problems.

Sequential combined hormone replacement therapy (HRT) is a medicine that contains the hormones oestrogen and progestogen. Sequential combined HRT involves taking oestrogen every day and taking progestogen for 10 to 14 days of each 28-day cycle. You'll usually have withdrawal bleeding at the end of each course of progestogen.  Oral oestrogen increases the risk of blood clots compared to transdermal options like patches or gels.  Tablets are processed by the liver, which may not suit women with liver disease or digestive issues.

Sequential combined HRT patches contain both oestrogen and progestogen, but the hormones are delivered in a cycle. Oestrogen is given continuously, while progestogen is added for part of the month. This mimics the natural menstrual cycle and usually results in a monthly withdrawal bleed. They are applied directly to the skin and changed once or twice weekly depending on the brand. The patches provide a two-in-one option without the need for separate tablets and does not have increase blood clot risk like tablets do. However they can cause skin irritation, and you may have issues with adhesion in hot weather or after bathing. Some women may also find them less discrete compared to pills or gels.

Tablets are one of the most common forms of HRT. You usually take them once a day. The fall in the amount of oestrogen your body produces can cause symptoms including hot flushes, low mood and vaginal dryness or irritation. Taking oestrogen as a form of HRT restores your levels of oestrogen and helps to relieve these symptoms. Taking tablets once a day may be the easiest way of having treatment. Some of the risks of HRT, such as blood clots, are higher with tablets than with patches, gel or spray (although the overall risk is still small). The IUS, such as a Minera coil, provides the progestogen still needed by gradually releasing it into your body from where its inserted in your womb. The Mirena coil can stay in place for up to 5 years and also acts as contraception. It can be a good option if you do not want to take or use a medicine every day, or have difficulties with other forms of progestogen. Implants such as the Mirena coil can cause abdominal pain and bleeding

Continuous combined oral HRT tablets contain both oestrogen and progestogen, taken together every day. This provides continuous protection for the womb lining and usually results in no monthly bleed. They are prescribed for women who still have a uterus and are postmenopausal. Proven to reduce hot flushes, night sweats, mood changes, and vaginal dryness, it is widely available and easy to adjust dosaes. There are possible side effects of  Breast tenderness, headaches, bloating, or mood changes and  Oral oestrogen increases the risk of blood clots compared to transdermal options like patches or gels.

Continuous combined HRT patches contain both oestrogen and progestogen. Unlike sequential patches, they release both hormones every day. This provides continuous protection for the womb lining and usually results in no monthly bleed. Its applied directly to the skina dn changes once or twice weekly. It releases oestrogen and progestogen steadily into the bloodstream through the skin.  Transdermal oestrogen does not increase blood clot risk in the same way oral tablets do and keeps a steady release of hormones compared to oral forms. Side effects can include Breast tenderness, headaches, mood changes, or bloating and redness or irratation aof the skin at the patch site.
